title: Sustainability & ESG in Projects
summary: The 2026 exam expects sustainability to be built into projects, not bolted on. It appears in planning, quality, procurement, compliance, and risk.

sections.heading: Why sustainability is on the exam now
sections.body:
Sustainability is the second trend PMI named in the 2026 update, and it is deliberately spread across the outline rather than isolated. You will see it as a critical information requirement during planning, as part of cost of quality, as a compliance category, and as a source of risk.

The expected mindset: sustainability is a real project requirement to plan for and measure, on par with scope, schedule, and cost.
sections.tip: If an option treats sustainability as optional or 'later', it is usually the weaker answer.

sections.heading: The triple bottom line
sections.body:
Sustainable project thinking balances three kinds of value, often called the triple bottom line:

- People (social) - communities, workers, health and safety, fairness.
- Planet (environmental) - emissions, waste, energy, resource use.
- Profit (economic) - the traditional financial result.

A strong project manager looks for solutions that protect all three rather than trading social or environmental harm for short-term cost or speed.
sections.tip: When choices pit cost or schedule against environmental or social harm, look for the option that integrates all three rather than sacrificing one.

sections.heading: Sustainability as a requirement and constraint
sections.body:
Treat sustainability requirements like any other: capture them with stakeholders, write them into scope and acceptance criteria, and reflect them in the plan and baselines. They then flow naturally into design decisions, supplier choices, and how you define 'done'.

Because it is a requirement, sustainability is also subject to change control - you don't quietly drop it under schedule pressure.
sections.tip: Build sustainability into the integrated plan and acceptance criteria early; retrofitting it late is costly and risky.

sections.heading: Sustainability, quality, and cost of quality
sections.body:
The 2026 outline pairs sustainability with cost of quality. Designing for sustainability - less waste, less rework, durable and efficient outputs - is a prevention cost that avoids larger failure costs later, the same logic as quality.

So sustainable choices and quality choices usually reinforce each other rather than compete.
sections.tip: Frame sustainability as prevention: a bit more up front avoids waste, rework, and remediation downstream.

sections.heading: Procurement and the supply chain
sections.body:
Much of a project's environmental and social footprint sits in its supply chain. Sustainable procurement means setting ESG criteria in selection, choosing responsible suppliers, and writing sustainability obligations into contracts - then verifying suppliers actually meet them.

Vendor sustainability performance becomes part of how you evaluate and manage suppliers.
sections.tip: Put sustainability into source-selection criteria and contract terms, not just into your own team's work.

sections.heading: Compliance and risk
sections.body:
Sustainability is now a compliance category alongside security and health and safety. The project manager confirms the relevant regulations and ESG-reporting obligations, classifies them, weighs the consequences of non-compliance (legal, financial, reputational), and acts to stay compliant.

It is also a risk source: environmental events, regulatory change, and reputational exposure are threats to identify, analyse, and respond to - and to communicate when a risk becomes an issue.
sections.tip: Missing a sustainability regulation is a compliance failure with legal and reputational fallout - confirm requirements early and track them.

sections.heading: Measuring sustainable value
sections.body: Value in the 2026 exam includes sustainability outcomes, not just financial return. Verify a measurement system is in place to track the sustainability benefits you promised - emissions avoided, waste reduced, social impact - and review them through delivery and into benefits realization after handover.
sections.tip: If you committed to a sustainability outcome, you need a metric for it - unmeasured benefits don't count.

source: Aligned with the PMP Examination Content Outline - July 2026 (sustainability across planning, quality, compliance, and risk) and PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition.
